identity.short_name: Even Betti sum via Alexander duality for a complement in R^3

subdomain(s): singular homology with coefficients in Q

problem_spec.summary: Given semialgebraic subsets of R^3 consisting of two disjoint spheres and an affine line tangent to one sphere, form their union U and study the complement S = R^3 \ U. Use one-point compactification to pass to S^3 and form A = closure(U) in S^3. Identify A up to homotopy as a disjoint union of a wedge S^2 ∨ S^1 and a sphere S^2, compute H_*(A;Q), then apply Alexander duality to obtain Betti numbers of S. The asked output is the even Betti sum b0(S)+b2(S).
givens: S1 is a sphere in R^3 given by an explicit quadratic equation.
S2 is a sphere in R^3 given by an explicit quadratic equation.
L is an affine line in R^3 given by two independent linear equations.
S1 and S2 are disjoint; L intersects S1 in exactly one point (tangency) and is disjoint from S2.
U = S1 ∪ S2 ∪ L and S = R^3 \ U.
Betti numbers b_k(S) are taken with rational coefficients.

parameter(s).name: semialgebraic_pieces
parameter(s).purpose: defines the union U whose complement is studied

parameter(s).name: intersection_pattern
parameter(s).domain: two disjoint spheres; one affine line tangent to exactly one sphere and disjoint from the other
parameter(s).purpose: forces a wedge-plus-disjoint-sphere homotopy model for the compactified closure A

parameter(s).name: coefficient_field
parameter(s).purpose: determines Betti numbers as vector-space dimensions (this instance uses Q)

parameter(s).name: output_linear_form
parameter(s).purpose: specifies which linear combination of Betti numbers is returned

invariant(s).name: compactification_equivalence
invariant(s).statement: For semialgebraic U ⊂ R^3, letting A be the closure of U in the one-point compactification S^3, the complement S = R^3 \ U is homotopy equivalent to S^3 \ A.

invariant(s).name: compactification_of_affine_line
invariant(s).statement: If L ⊂ R^3 is an affine line (homeomorphic to R), then its closure in S^3 is homeomorphic to S^1.

invariant(s).name: alexander_duality_relation
invariant(s).statement: Reduced cohomology satisfies  \tilde H^i(S^3 \ A; k) ≅ \tilde H_{2-i}(A; k)  for i=0,1,2 over a field k.

essential_assumption(s): The given sets are semialgebraic (locally contractible), so Alexander duality applies to A ⊂ S^3.
The intersection pattern (disjointness and single-point tangency) holds; otherwise the homotopy type of A may change.
Coefficients are taken in a field (this instance uses Q), so Betti numbers are dimensions of vector spaces.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The explicit defining equations uniquely determine the intersection graph: S1 ∩ S2 = ∅, L ∩ S1 is one point, and L ∩ S2 = ∅. Under one-point compactification, L closes to S^1, so A is homotopy equivalent to (S^2 ∨ S^1) ⊔ S^2. This homotopy type uniquely determines H_*(A) over Q, and Alexander duality uniquely determines b0(S) and b2(S), hence b0(S)+b2(S) is uniquely determined.

technique(s).name: compactify_and_replace_complement
technique(s).purpose: replace S = R^3 \ U by S^3 \ A via one-point compactification

technique(s).name: analyze_intersection_pattern
technique(s).purpose: solve intersection conditions to determine the intersection graph of the pieces and their closures

technique(s).name: model_closure_as_wedge_and_disjoint_union
technique(s).purpose: identify A up to homotopy using wedge (contractible intersection) and disjoint-union decomposition

technique(s).name: compute_homology_from_models
technique(s).purpose: compute H_*(A) using standard homology of S^1, S^2, and behavior under wedge/disjoint unions

technique(s).name: apply_Alexander_duality_and_extract_Betti
technique(s).purpose: apply Alexander duality and universal coefficients (over Q) to obtain Betti numbers of S

theorem(s).name: Mayer_Vietoris
theorem(s).role: computes homology of unions from homology of parts and intersections (used to justify wedge modeling)

theorem(s).name: wedge_from_contractible_intersection
theorem(s).role: if X and Y are path-connected and X∩Y is contractible, then X∪Y ≃ X∨Y

theorem(s).name: homology_of_spheres_and_circles
theorem(s).role: provides H_*(S^1;k) and H_*(S^2;k) to compute H_*(A)

theorem(s).name: universal_coefficients_over_Q
theorem(s).role: ensures ranks of H^i and H_i agree over Q

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: compute topology of a complement by dualizing the homology of the compactified obstacle set

critical_trick(s): Pass from R^3 to S^3 via one-point compactification so the obstacle becomes compact.
Recognize an affine line compactifies to S^1 and a one-point intersection with a sphere yields a wedge S^2 ∨ S^1.
Use Alexander duality to read b0 and b2 of the complement from \tilde H2(A) and \tilde H0(A).

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: exact solving of polynomial systems to verify intersection/tangency and disjointness for concrete instances

library(s).name: sage
library(s).reason: optional independent homology verification from a simplicial/CW model of the identified homotopy type

import_hint(s): from sympy import symbols, Eq, solve

task(s).name: verify_intersection_pattern
task(s).reason: confirm S1∩S2=∅, L∩S1 is exactly one point, and L∩S2=∅

task(s).name: optional_homology_rank_check
task(s).reason: verify the homology ranks of (S^2 ∨ S^1) ⊔ S^2 computationally as a sanity check
